data.summary: Appended research findings (iteration 0)

Three adversarial gaps remain unaddressed. (1) The 'strictly harder than CFL' sub-claim for HTML5 <script>/<template> has zero published formal-language proof — the prior evidence's own Dependency Auditor explicitly flags it as an 'unvalidated dependency'; no pumping-lemma-for-CFLs argument or Chomsky-reduction appears in any cited source. data.summary: The claim has two tiers of support. Tier 1 — HTML is not a regular language — is proven (not merely evidenced) by 4 independent Level-2 sources via the pumping lemma: balanced tag pairs at arbitrary depth are isomorphic to {a^n b^n}, which no finite automaton can recognise. Tier 2 — HTML is at least context-free — is well-supported by the Chomsky hierarchy (Level-2 sources: Fitch & Friederici 2012, CMU 15-411 2024, ODU CS390) showing unbounded nesting requires pushdown-automaton power. Tier 3 — HTML5 `<script>`/`<template>` is *strictly harder* than CFL — remains an acknowledged open dependency: architecturally motivated (the WHATWG tokenizer switches between RAWTEXT/script-data states based on element name, a context-sensitive production rule), but no published formal Chomsky-reduction proof exists; the dependency_auditor's concern here is valid and the 'strictly harder' qualifier should be characterised as 'architecturally context-sensitive' rather than formally proven Type-1. The dependency_auditor's 'total transducer' objection (WHATWG accepts all strings, so its language is trivially Σ* = regular) is correctly scoped away: the claim applies to the normative language of *well-formed* HTML documents, not to WHATWG's error-recovery transducer mapping Σ* to DOM trees. Verdict: 7 sources across 3 hierarchy levels, core non-regularity claim is airtight, CFL floor is strongly supported, 'strictly harder' qualifier is defensible but unproven.

This is the strongest formal argument: it constitutes a proof, not merely evidence.

The inclusions are strict and mathematically proven — a context-free language that is not regular exists (e.g. a^n b^n), so the classes are distinct.

sources_cited.finding: Context-free grammars admit efficient LL/LR/Earley parsing; finite automata (Type 3) cannot produce recursive derivation trees required by nested markup. Critically, Chomsky classification presupposes a string-membership decision problem — a prerequisite the WHATWG HTML5 total-transducer parser does not satisfy.

sources_cited.title: Old Dominion University CS390, Non-Regular Languages (course notes)
sources_cited.finding: Finite automata have only finite state memory and cannot count unbounded quantities; any language requiring recognition of unbounded balanced nesting — directly the HTML tag-pairing problem — exceeds the power of any DFA.

sources_cited.title: John D. Cook, Can Regular Expressions Parse HTML or Not? (2013)
sources_cited.finding: Classical Kleene-closure regular expressions describe exactly Type-3 languages; HTML with arbitrary nesting is Type-2, so classical regex cannot parse it — but modern PCRE engines with recursive subpatterns exceed formal Type-3, a terminological conflation the claim does not resolve.

sources_cited.title: Neil Madden, Why You Really Can Parse HTML (and Anything Else) with Regular Expressions (2019)
sources_cited.finding: Modern PCRE regex engines with recursive subpatterns can match arbitrarily nested structures, directly contradicting the colloquial 'cannot parse HTML with regex' form of the claim while leaving the formal Type-3 mathematical claim intact.

sources_cited.title: Hacker News, HTML is a Chomsky Type 2 (Context-Free) Grammar — Community Discussion (2021)
sources_cited.finding: Expert community confirms arbitrary nested HTML requires Type-2 power, but flags that real browsers cap nesting at a finite constant K — making that finite-depth sublanguage technically regular — and that no participant cites a formal proof placing HTML5 strictly above Type 2.

sources_cited.title: Jeff Atwood, Parsing HTML The Cthulhu Way, Coding Horror (2009)
sources_cited.finding: Practitioner post arguing that HTML's nested, recursive tag structure cannot be reliably handled by regex and requires a real HTML parser, consistent with the formal non-regularity result and illustrating the practical downstream impact on security tooling.

data.exchange_summary: [Round 1.0] skeptic: Verdict: UNADDRESSED_COUNTERARGUMENT. 3 critical unaddressed counterarguments persist: (1) The WHATWG HTML5 normative specification defines a total transducer (never rejects any input — parse errors are reported but parsing continues to produce a DOM), making the 'language of HTML5' trivially Σ* and rendering Chomsky classification inapplicable to the normative spec; (2) The 'strictly harder than CFL' sub-claim for `<script>`/`<template>` remains formally unproven in any cited source — 0 published Chomsky-reduction proofs exist, only procedural tokenizer architecture evidence; (3) real browsers cap nesting depth at a finite constant K, making the actually-implemented language technically recognizable by a DFA (trivially regular), meaning the pumping-lemma argument applies only to an infinite-depth idealization no real document can instantiate. The single strongest reason to doubt the claim: the WHATWG HTML5 parser is specified as a total function over all strings, so there is no rejection predicate — the Chomsky hierarchy framework the claim invokes does not apply to the normative WHATWG definition of HTML5 at all. | [Round 1.0] champion: Verdict: DEFENSIBLE_WITH_CAVEATS. The claim has two tiers of support. Tier 1 — HTML is not a regular language — is proven (not merely evidenced) by 4 independent Level-2 sources via the pumping lemma: balanced tag pairs at arbitrary depth are isomorphic to {a^n b^n}, which no finite automaton can recognise. Tier 2 — HTML is at least context-free — is well-supported by the Chomsky hierarchy (Level-2 sources: Fitch & Friederici 2012, CMU 15-411 2024, ODU CS390) showing unbounded nesting requires pushdown-automaton power. Tier 3 — HTML5 `<script>`/`<template>` is *strictly harder* than CFL — remains an acknowledged open dependency: architecturally motivated (the WHATWG tokenizer switches between RAWTEXT/script-data states based on element name, a context-sensitive production rule), but no published formal Chomsky-reduction proof exists; the dependency_auditor's concern here is valid and the 'strictly harder' qualifier should be characterised as 'architecturally context-sensitive' rather than formally proven Type-1. The dependency_auditor's 'total transducer' objection (WHATWG accepts all strings, so its language is trivially Σ* = regular) is correctly scoped away: the claim applies to the normative language of *well-formed* HTML documents, not to WHATWG's error-recovery transducer mapping Σ* to DOM trees. Verdict: 7 sources across 3 hierarchy levels, core non-regularity claim is airtight, CFL floor is strongly supported, 'strictly harder' qualifier is defensible but unproven. | [Round 1.5] skeptic: Responding to DEFENSIBLE_WITH_CAVEATS. The single strongest reason to doubt the claim: the WHATWG HTML5 parser is specified as a total function over all strings, so there is no rejection predicate — the Chomsky hierarchy framework the claim invokes does not apply to the normative WHATWG definition of HTML5 at all. | [Round 1.0] pragmatist: Verdict: DIRECTIONALLY_CORRECT. The non-regularity sub-claim has direct, high-stakes practical significance: it is the formal theoretical basis for the industry consensus that regex-based HTML sanitizers produce exploitable XSS bypasses and must be replaced by parser-backed tools (html5lib, lxml, DOMPurify). The 'strictly harder than CFL for HTML5 <script>/<template>' sub-claim is architecturally real — every production browser implements explicit tokenizer mode-switching for these elements — but carries no formal published proof above Type 2, so it should not be shipped as a settled formal-language result; the claim as bundled is directionally correct but too imprecise at the HTML5 sub-claim to be production-ready.

[Round 1.0] simplifier: Verdict: OVERCOMPLICATED. The claim bundles 3 sub-claims of unequal evidentiary strength into one sentence: (1) HTML ≠ regular — fully proven at Level 2 via pumping lemma across 4 independent sources; (2) HTML ≥ CFL — well-supported by theory but not formally proven for the WHATWG Living Standard, which is a total transducer over Σ* rather than a membership-deciding acceptor; (3) HTML5 `<script>`/`<template>` pushes HTML strictly above Type 2 — has zero formal published proof, only procedural tokenizer-architecture evidence. The scope is overcomplicated: the only defensible single-sentence claim is the non-regularity of HTML's balanced-tag sublanguage; the 'strictly harder than CFL' parenthetical introduces an unvalidated dependency that the dependency_auditor correctly flagged and that no retrieved source resolves. | [Round 1.0] impact_assessor: Verdict: CRITICAL_PATH. This cell is **FOUNDATION_STONE** with blast radius **HIGH (≥6 downstream dependencies)**: the non-regularity sub-claim underpins (1) all security guidance mandating DOM-based HTML sanitizers over regex (OWASP, XSS defence), (2) justification for dedicated parser libraries (html5lib, BeautifulSoup, browser engines) over lexers, (3) formal CS pedagogy across compiler-design courses (CMU 15-411, ODU CS390) using HTML as the canonical CFL-not-regular example, (4) scraping and ETL tool selection guidance, (5) interpretation of CVEs in regex-based sanitizers, and (6) the 'strictly harder than CFL' sub-claim for HTML5 `<script>`/`<template>` which drives parser-security threat modelling. If the claim is wrong — specifically if HTML were shown to be regular under a well-defined formalism — the formal justification for prohibiting regex-based sanitizers in security-critical applications collapses, invalidating the theoretical grounding of XSS defences across web security curricula and tooling.
